summary: Jeff Atwood highlights the book 'Peopleware' after it was featured on Kevin Kelly's Cool Tools list. He endorses it as an essential resource for anyone managing projects or teams in software development. The post shares Kelly's review, which praises the book's practical wisdom on team productivity, the myth of overtime, and creating effective work environments. Atwood strongly recommends the book as a must-read for developers and project managers.
key_points: Peopleware has been a quiet but influential guide for Silicon Valley startups and successful projects for over 20 years
The book focuses on optimizing people rather than time management, addressing team dynamics, workspace needs, and the dangers of overtime
It targets project-level management rather than large organizational concerns, making it practical for hands-on work
The book's lessons on team cohesion, closure ceremonies, and physical workspace remain relevant decades after publication
money_quotes: Hard-won wisdom fills this small book: How to create a team, place, or company that is productive.
I learned about the myth of overtime, the need for closure and ceremonies, how teams jell, and why everyone should and can have a window.
I first read it decades ago and re-read it every time I embark on anything involving more than one person and several years of my life.
The message in the book touts productivity, without ever mentioning the dreary idea of time management.
It's more about optimizing people, and thus the title, Peopleware.
If you don't have a copy of Peopleware yet, what are you waiting for?
key_insight: Sustainable software project success comes from optimizing people and team dynamics rather than managing time or enforcing overtime.
